detail.summary: Ikigai (生き甲斐) fuses 'life' and 'to be worthwhile.' It sits at the intersection of what you love, what you're good at, what the world needs, and what you can be paid for — roughly, 'the happiness of always being busy.'
points: Everyone's ikigai differs, but all of us search for meaning
Finding it takes a patient inward search
Based on 100 interviews with centenarians in Ogimi, Okinawa

detail.summary: A reason for being — the thing that gives meaning to your days and keeps you going until the very end.
points: Feeling connected to meaning = living more fully
People with a clear ikigai pursue their passion no matter what

detail.summary: Love × skill × world's need × livelihood. Where all four overlap, ikigai lives.
The one thing we all share is the search itself. Discovering your ikigai is the first mission.
A short code the Okinawan elders live by — small daily habits, not grand gestures.
Keep doing the things you love. Purpose doesn't have a finish line.
Stop before you're full. Don't fill your stomach to capacity.
Don't dwell on the past or fret the future. Be here, now.
Premature ageing is strongly tied to stress and existential drift.
points: Existential crisis: doing what others expect, not what you want
Filling the gap with money or pleasure numbs the senses
Give up what you love → lose your purpose

detail.summary: Research links premature ageing to chronic stress.
Doing what you're told or what others do, instead of what you want, drains meaning.
Flow is total immersion — energised focus and enjoyment in the activity itself.
points: Focus on one thing at a time
Prefer flow over quick immediate pleasure
The happiest people spend the most time in flow — not those who achieve most

detail.summary: Single-tasking may be the single biggest factor in reaching flow.
Turning ordinary tasks into something enjoyable is key to being happy.
Being in a hurry is inversely proportional to quality of life. 'Walk slowly and you'll go far.'
points: Leave urgency behind — time gains new meaning
Never stop doing what you love
Slow living means dropping hurry, not busyness

detail.summary: You can be fully occupied with beloved work without ever rushing.
The elders of Ogimi, asked how to live long and happy, answered plainly.
Good company sustains you into old age.
Keep both engaged and you'll be here a long time.
